_about: Quick Drills — interactive scenario training served in the Stage 4 / Quick Drills section of the staff training dashboard.

_agent_instructions.purpose: This file contains all Quick Drills scenario content for three hospitality modules: Bartending, Sales and Upselling, and Shift Leadership (Management). Each scenario presents a realistic workplace situation and gives the staff member three pill options to tap. Two pills are marked positive (correct approach) and one is marked negative (what to avoid).
_agent_instructions.how_to_add: Add new scenarios to any module by appending objects to the relevant module array. Each scenario must have: 'text' (the situation description, 1-3 sentences, present tense, second person — 'You have...' or 'A guest...'), and 'pills' (exactly 3 objects, each with 'intent' (2-5 word label), 'text' (the specific action, 8-20 words), and 'positive' (true or false)). Always have exactly 2 positive pills and 1 negative pill per scenario.
_agent_instructions.tone: Direct, practical, Australian hospitality context. No academic language. Write like a senior hospo professional briefing their team. Present tense, active voice.
_agent_instructions.quality_bar: Every scenario should describe a real situation that actually happens on a shift. The negative pill should be a tempting mistake — not obviously wrong — something a nervous or untrained staff member might actually do.
_agent_instructions.target_count_per_module: 20 scenarios each. Currently: Bartending 10, Sales 10, Management 20. Priority: expand Bartending and Sales to 20 each.

bartending._context: Bar craft, cocktail technique, and RSA compliance. Australian-specific: 30ml standard nip, RSA (Responsible Service of Alcohol) certification is legally required in all states, WHS (Work Health and Safety) replaces OHS. Scenarios cover: cocktail building technique (shaking vs stirring, dilution, ice types, egg white dry shake), classic recipes (Espresso Martini, Negroni, Martini, Daiquiri, Mojito, Sazerac, Old Fashioned, Highball, Margarita), spirits knowledge (Bourbon vs Rye, vermouth storage, Four Pillars Gin, Starward Whisky), RSA refusal protocol, guest recommendations, bar workflow and sequencing under pressure, Guinness two-part pour, and mise en place.

scenarios.text: The 'Surprise Me' guest says they like gin but are tired of a G&T. How do you ask clarifying questions, make a tailored recommendation, and then build it confidently?

pills.text: Ask preferred flavour profile (citrus, herbal, bitter, sweet)
Suggest one drink and explain why it fits
Make any gin cocktail without asking questions

scenarios.text: You have three orders at once: Mojito, Guinness, and house Chardonnay. Describe your exact workflow and mise-en-place so speed does not damage quality.

pills.text: Start Guinness first, build Mojito while it settles, then pour wine
Set up glassware and garnish before touching bottles
Bounce between drinks without a plan

scenarios.text: A guest asks for a Brandy Frappé. Explain the recipe, correct glassware, and key technique to prepare this classic correctly.

pills.text: Confirm ingredients and method before preparing
Tell the guest what style they can expect (refreshing/brandy-led)
Make a random brandy drink and call it a Frappé

scenarios.text: A guest wants a Margarita made with Scotch. How do you guide them professionally toward a better fit while still making them feel heard?

pills.text: Explain why Scotch shifts the profile and suggest a Penicillin or Whisky Sour
Offer a custom riff with clear flavour expectations
Tell them that request is wrong and refuse abruptly

scenarios.text: It is 10 minutes to closing and a group asks for six complicated cocktails. How do you balance hospitality, speed, and team communication?

pills.text: Acknowledge warmly, explain realistic options, and offer a streamlined recommendation
Split prep tasks and keep ticket communication tight
Accept everything silently and let service collapse

scenarios.text: A guest wants to understand Bourbon vs Rye through a three-whiskey flight. What do you choose and how do you explain each pour?

pills.text: Pick one approachable bourbon, one high-rye bourbon, and one rye
Highlight sweetness vs spice, mash bill, and finish
Pour any three and let them figure it out

scenarios.text: A guest asks for a 'strong but smooth' cocktail. How do you recommend something that matches both requests without overwhelming them?

pills.text: Offer spirit-forward options with softer texture (e.g. Old Fashioned variation)
Explain alcohol intensity vs perceived smoothness
Automatically serve the highest-ABV drink

scenarios.text: A guest asks why your Martini tastes better than usual. How do you explain vermouth handling, brand choice, and technique in a way that builds trust?

pills.text: Explain chilled glass, fresh vermouth storage, dilution control, and proper stirring
Translate technique into guest-facing quality language
Say it is just the house style with no explanation

scenarios.text: You believe a guest is intoxicated but they insist on another round of shots for the group. How do you decline service with tact and safety focus?

pills.text: Decline politely, offer water/food, and involve support if needed
Keep tone neutral and avoid confrontation in front of others
Serve the shots anyway to keep them happy

scenarios.text: A guest orders a Sazerac. Walk through the exact spec, including the rinse, spirit choice, and garnish protocol.

pills.text: Use correct base spirit, proper absinthe rinse, and lemon peel expression
Describe glass prep, dilution, and service language
Treat it like a generic stirred whiskey cocktail

sales._context: Professional sales and upselling techniques for hospitality floor staff. Australian restaurant and bar context. Covers: timing suggestions correctly (starters before mains, dessert at close), framing upsells as low-commitment and guest-centric, premium spirit and wine upsells, bottle vs individual glass recommendations, occasion-based selling (birthdays, anniversaries, celebrations), social proof language ('other guests tonight have been loving...'), pairing recommendations, reading when NOT to upsell (rushed guests, closed-off body language), closing techniques, and pre-shift specials preparation. The key principle: upsells should feel helpful and natural, not scripted or pushy.

scenarios.text: A couple sits at the bar and orders two cocktails but has not opened the food menu. How do you introduce a snack or small plate in a way that feels helpful rather than pushy?

pills.text: Suggest a snack that complements the cocktails they chose
Frame it as something easy to share while they settle in
Tell them they should really order food immediately

scenarios.text: A table of eight is undecided and still reading menus. How do you recommend two appetizers for the table while positioning them as the best way to start?

pills.text: Recommend two crowd-pleasers and explain why they work for sharing
Frame the starters as something to enjoy while mains are decided
Wait for them to ask for appetizer ideas

scenarios.text: A guest orders the sirloin, but your ribeye is the stronger premium sell. How do you describe the ribeye so the upsell feels natural and justified?

pills.text: Explain the richer marbling, flavour and eating experience
Keep the guest in control while highlighting the upgrade clearly
Tell them the sirloin is not worth ordering

scenarios.text: A guest asks, 'What's good tonight?' How do you answer by spotlighting one chef's special and pairing it with a wine or cocktail recommendation?

pills.text: Recommend one standout special and explain why it is performing well
Attach a concrete beverage match to lift the overall order
Say everything is good and leave it at that

scenarios.text: A table says they are too full for dessert, but you know they are celebrating a birthday. How do you pivot to a shared dessert without sounding salesy?

pills.text: Suggest one dessert to share as a small birthday moment
Frame it as a few bites rather than a full extra course
Insist they should all have a dessert each

scenarios.text: A guest orders an espresso. How do you pair it immediately with a small dessert like tiramisu or a cookie plate as the perfect companion?

pills.text: Link the espresso to a small dessert as a classic finishing combination
Offer a light option that feels easy to say yes to
Serve the coffee and move on without mentioning dessert

scenarios.text: A guest points to the house wine. How do you describe it briefly and then offer a taste of the next-tier premium glass without sounding pushy?

pills.text: Confirm the house option, then mention a premium by-the-glass upgrade with one clear benefit
Offer a taste to lower the decision risk
Tell them the house wine is not very good

scenarios.text: A guest orders a well vodka soda. How do you suggest a premium vodka and explain why it will make the drink smoother and cleaner?

pills.text: Highlight smoother finish and cleaner taste in the same serve
Recommend one specific premium vodka rather than a generic upsell
Ask 'Do you want premium?' without context

scenarios.text: A table of four orders four different glasses of wine. How do you suggest a bottle they could all enjoy, while making the value case clearly?

pills.text: Show how the bottle compares favourably against four individual glasses
Frame the bottle as a better table experience, not just a cheaper option
List too many bottle options and confuse the table

scenarios.text: You overhear that a table is celebrating an anniversary. How do you acknowledge it and suggest Champagne or a special dessert so it feels personal rather than scripted?

pills.text: Acknowledge the occasion warmly and make one tailored suggestion
Position the drink or dessert as part of the celebration
Push the most expensive Champagne immediately

management._context: Shift leadership and venue management. Australian-specific: Fair Work Act, Hospitality Industry (General) Award, WHS, RSA management obligations, visa sponsorship considerations. Covers: staff progression paths and milestone check-ins, pre-shift huddles (tight and focused, max 5 minutes), retention strategies (resignations as system feedback), coaching vs accountability (capability gap vs disengagement), crisis leadership (choose the calm trusted person, not longest tenure), delegation with outcome-based guardrails, financial literacy for junior managers (labour %, GP, average spend per cover), FOH-kitchen conflict resolution (reset around shared guest outcomes), onboarding philosophy (explain the 'why' not just the tasks), roster sustainability under Fair Work obligations, peer recognition systems, and proactive RSA management.

scenarios.text: A junior staff member shows great attitude but limited experience and has been stuck running food for months. How do you create a stepped progression plan that keeps them engaged and gives them a path forward?

pills.text: Set clear next skills, shadow shifts, and milestone check-ins
Explain how good attitude and consistency lead to expanded responsibility
Keep them doing the same support role without a development plan

scenarios.text: You have an experienced senior team member who is technically brilliant but hesitant to step into leadership. How do you back them and build their confidence?

pills.text: Name the leadership traits you already see and offer a supported first step
Give them small leadership moments before a formal title change
Tell them to step up immediately or miss their chance

scenarios.text: Your company wants to implement structured progression plans like a formal development program. How do you introduce it to the team and get buy-in from sceptical staff?

pills.text: Connect the program to pay growth, confidence, and future opportunities
Help each person choose individual goals rather than forcing a generic plan
Roll it out as mandatory admin with no explanation

scenarios.text: A talented overseas staff member is invaluable, but their visa expires in 8 months. How do you start early conversations about sponsorship and a realistic pathway?

pills.text: Open the conversation well before deadlines and explain business realities honestly
Discuss what the venue needs and what support the employee would need to stay
Wait until the visa is nearly over before raising it

scenarios.text: You want to build a culture of reflection and continuous improvement. How do you implement a simple weekly 'I learned' practice without making it feel like homework?

pills.text: Ask each person to share one practical lesson from the week in a quick huddle
Model it yourself so the practice feels safe and useful
Turn it into a formal written assignment every week

scenarios.text: A strong staff member resigns for only slightly more pay, saying they did not feel valued or see growth opportunities. How do you use that feedback before others follow?

pills.text: Review recognition, progression, and manager communication patterns immediately
Hold retention conversations with your key team members now
Assume they only left for money and change nothing

scenarios.text: You want to improve morale and alignment with 5-minute pre-shift check-ins. How do you make these huddles useful rather than another empty meeting?

pills.text: Recognise wins, flag priorities, and align on the service goal of the shift
Use a consistent but brief format that includes team energy, not just tasks
Turn it into a long lecture before every shift

scenarios.text: Your team has been working excessive hours during a busy period. How do you restructure rosters to protect work-life balance without losing service standards?

pills.text: Reduce repeated overloading, rotate tough shifts, and identify fatigue risks early
Explain that sustainable rosters support better service and lower turnover
Keep stretching the same reliable staff because they can handle it

scenarios.text: You want a simple, cost-free peer recognition system. How do you set it up so it feels authentic and builds appreciation rather than awkwardness?

pills.text: Ask for concrete examples of helpful actions rather than generic praise
Use a quick weekly nomination moment with genuine manager follow-through
Require everyone to nominate someone every shift

scenarios.text: Owners see team building as an expense rather than an investment. How do you make the case for allocating budget to retention-focused culture activities?

pills.text: Tie culture spend to retention, recruitment cost, and service consistency
Explain how stronger loyalty reduces turnover disruption and training drag
Argue that team bonding is just nice to have

scenarios.text: A junior staff member apologises to a customer for a senior colleague's difficult manner during training. How do you coach both staff members to present a united front?

pills.text: Address the senior's warmth and the junior's front-of-house professionalism separately
Reinforce that internal frustrations should never be offloaded onto customers
Treat it as personality clash and leave it unresolved

scenarios.text: Tension is building between front-of-house and kitchen, with blame-shifting during busy services. How do you improve communication before it becomes toxic?

pills.text: Bring both teams together around service outcomes rather than departmental blame
Agree clearer call times, escalation rules, and post-service debriefs
Back one department publicly and hope the other adapts

scenarios.text: A sudden crisis happens while you are off-site. How do you identify and empower the right interim leader from the floor?

pills.text: Pick the person others naturally follow under pressure, not just the loudest voice
Give explicit decision rights and support boundaries before stepping away
Appoint the longest-serving person without considering trust or leadership

scenarios.text: You have recently been promoted and keep micromanaging former peers. How do you learn to delegate properly without losing standards?

pills.text: Set the outcome, guardrails, and follow-up point instead of controlling each step
Accept that some variation is part of development and not always failure
Keep taking tasks back whenever they are not done your exact way

scenarios.text: During a busy crisis, one staff member froze or abandoned their post. After service, how do you distinguish between someone who tried but failed and someone who gave up?

pills.text: Ask what they saw, thought, and attempted before deciding the response
Use coaching for capability gaps and firmer accountability for disengagement
Issue the same warning without understanding what happened

scenarios.text: A new staff member is unsure about gaming room responsible service requirements and a patron is showing early distress. How do you turn this into a teaching moment while staying compliant?

pills.text: Step in immediately, support the patron appropriately, and explain the why afterward
Use the live situation to reinforce duty of care and procedure
Wait until later and hope the issue settles on its own

scenarios.text: A regular guest is becoming intoxicated, but the team likes them and a junior staff member is unsure how to cut them off. How do you support the staff member and handle the guest?

pills.text: Step in visibly, reinforce the decision, and keep the interaction respectful
Debrief the incident to strengthen future confidence and consistency
Let the regular slide because they are well-known

scenarios.text: You are onboarding new staff and want to start with the venue's 'why' instead of only task lists. How do you do that in a practical, meaningful way?

pills.text: Explain the service philosophy and how it shapes everyday decisions on the floor
Use real examples of what great service looks like in your venue
Skip the purpose and only cover tasks and rules

scenarios.text: You want to give your management team more visibility over business finances and decisions. How do you introduce financial literacy without overwhelming them?

pills.text: Focus on a few useful metrics like labour, margin, and average spend first
Translate numbers into operational choices they can actually influence
Expose full financial detail all at once without context

scenarios.text: You want junior staff to build better relationships with regulars. How do you introduce a simple 'regulars file' system without making it creepy or overly formal?

pills.text: Track useful service notes like names, favourite orders, and harmless preferences
Explain how it helps newer staff deliver warmer, more confident service
Encourage staff to record overly personal details about guests
